Some people say HTML is a programming language, others say it isn't. Let's talk about which side is right and why.
Quick recap on what a programming language actually is.

In short, a programming language is a language used to communicate with a computer. It typically gives you syntax for things like variables, conditionals, loops, functions — the basic building blocks.
So the basic criterion: if it has syntax that lets you tell the computer to do things, it counts as a programming language.
So, is HTML a programming language?
Onto the actual question — is HTML a programming language? Bottom line first:
Why? First, Wikipedia's definition of HTML:
HyperText Markup Language (HTML) is the standard markup language for documents designed to be displayed in a web browser.
HTML is described as a markup language used to build web pages, not a programming language. That's reason one.
You might be thinking — "but it does communicate with the computer, right?"
Sort of. But HTML is only telling the browser things like "I have an

tag, please render this text as a top-level heading" or "I have an image, please display it". It's instructing the browser on what to display, and the browser follows those instructions. That's HTML's basic model.
This feels 87% like programming, but real programming languages — like JavaScript — give you variables, conditionals, and loops:
You can clearly see the difference vs HTML — in HTML you can't use variables, conditionals, or loops at all. That's reason two.
Programming languages have variables, conditionals, and loops. HTML doesn't.
Programming languages tell the computer to do things. HTML just tells the browser how to display things.

You might be wondering why template languages like EJS, Pug, etc. are categorised as programming languages. That's because they use actual programming languages to generate HTML, so they themselves count.
HTML isn't a programming language, but template languages are
